a) I've recently started doing the LBRP daily, and noticed a very odd effect: my ability to stand energy drinks has vanished. In the space of a few days they went from a normal part of my life to being viscerally unpleasant, tasting unpleasantly chemical, and leaving a very nasty feeling when I actually drank one. This has given me a huge problem, because I've suddenly cut down on my caffeine (and other stuff), and have had withdrawal symptoms, but it also suggests a troubling possibility that there is something unpleasant here.

Banishing has not affected my ability to enjoy eating various other forms of processed foods, nor other forms of caffeine (including straight caffeine pills, tea, and coffee), nor impacted other mind-altering substances, such as alcohol, tobacco, vaping, kratom. It seems this effect is unique to energy drinks.

Do you know if there is some link between energy drinks and noxious energies of some kind? If so, it's mildly alarming how popular these drinks are....

b) There is a company called 2wai that is making news due to a remarkably creepy advertisement that has me thinking about a really creepy possibility. The advertisement was of a child talking to "grandma", updating her with his life as he aged. Then it turned out that it was not her, but a model her daughter had built by an AI company before she died.

This sort of digital necromancy seems very creepy to me, and raises some profoundly troubling thoughts that tie into Magic Monday (and a galaxy more that don't!). The first is that it's not at all clear to me that this sort of tech, assuming it works, would be safe, for metaphysical reasons: it seems to me it could run the risk of making contact with beings we'd rather not interact with. It could also, depending on what sort of effects this has, disturb and bother the dead.

Then the very dark thought that occurred to me is that the deceased souls of all these people who are making this nightmare world might, when they wake up on the other side and realize they will have to face the consequences of their actions, might end up trying to inhabit these models, their own or someone else's, to avoid passing on. In a twisted sort of way, then, all this talk of uploading our minds to machines might actually be possible: but it would almost certainly not be an existence any of us would want, and it would not be at all what anyone expects. It would actually probably be hellish beyond imagination, but the quest for immortality has often lead that way.

I have no idea if this would be possible, but if it is...

replies.content:
Also, I'd like to remind everyone discussing hypotheticals involving more mechanically-fine-grained digital emulations of the physics of people's brains -- e.g. 3bmend_135 and JMG above and Christophe below -- that if a population of emulated brains behaves in any way that is statistically distinguishable from the corresponding behaviors of a corresponding population of real brains (I mean, once one has barred fallacies of post-hoc analysis, e.g. using Bonferroni corrections or Bayesian hyperprior regularization), then the event of those brains behaving in those ways would constitute a natural experiment in parapsychology, and so the existence of any such discrepancy would ipso facto constitute a positive finding in the parapsychology experiment thus constituted. A century of experience has shown that the ability to obtain consistent otherwise-seemingly-unequivocal positive findings in any given paradigm of parapsychology experimentation has always evaporated after, at most, a few decades. (This is the largest-scale version of the famous "decline effect" in parapsychology, which can be thought of as sort of a flipside of the idea of "beginner's luck".)

Sometimes this evaporation rugpulls away what might have seemed like basic certainties about the supernatural -- for example, "well, even if my aura sight was on the fritz, it at least wouldn't falsely and confidently perceive the extending aura of a person standing behind all these opaque partitions in this simple test chamber when at most half of them have someone behind them" -- in the process. Yes, that example has a confounding alternative explanation like "well we didn't think of this in advance, but maybe astral-planar connections caused by the ritual of experimental blinding are conductive of auras between the experimental group of partitions that did have people behind them and the control group of partitions that didn't", but that's part of the point. The very fact that the supernatural had to, as if by a pre-arranged harmony, have already positioned itself so as to make sure that there would be a confounding alternative explanation for this negative finding in parapsychology that would seem "natural" to believers, while still preserving the plausibility of the explanation about supernatural perceptions being entirely confabulated that would have seemed natural for unbelievers, needs to be understood as a possible warning shot that there seems to be a transhumanly clever and far-seeing mind at work here, arranging entirely-innocently-organically-emergent-seeming ways for potentially literally any apparent certainty about the supernatural to get rugpulled out from under you, as soon as you try to operationalize that seeming certainty in a way that would clearly contradict materialism's predictions to a general global audience with no room for confounding alternate explanations to interpret the contradiction away.

Of course "brain emulations" might somehow prove to be the precise point at which the rugpuller decides instead to rugpull its own self, in which case your speculations might go through as stated. But it doesn't seem to me as though your investments of normative emotion (at least emotions about practical normativity, but probably also moral normativity and the virtuousness/unvirtuousness of different cultural factions) and confidence around these speculations are structured in a way that is compatible with "following the grain" of the way this universe works, if it's the kind of universe where that kind of rugpulling to put escape clauses in counter-materialist predictions can and does happen constantly.

To spell it out in this case: The obvious neutral extrapolation from the observed evidence to date is that, whatever supernatural discrepancies there are that might show up involving brain emulations, such as brain emulations having observably demonic-puppeteering-like effects on this world, such discrepancies can only persist in a large-scale outwardly-at-first-glance-unambiguous-seeming form for, at most, a few decades.

If you invest your emotions of confidence into the prediction of a form of demonic-puppeteering of brain emulations or whatever, and the hypothesized phenomenon you're basing that prediction on wouldn't have a natural cutoff on the timescale of a few decades (such that, as that timescale passes by, the emulated brains would reliably start instead only having effects on the world that weren't clearly distinguishable from those that normal brains would have had), and you don't have a good reason why the parapsychology rugpulling phenomenon should reliably suddenly stop being applicable there, then you've stopped short of trying to make an account of the enormous pile of evidence from parapsychology. In which case you should on average expect, in turn, that your emotions of confidence will end up feeling rugpulledly betrayed, and the normativities to which those feelings are attached will (on average) suddenly rugpulledly turn into one of those "things ill done and done to others' harm / Which once you took for exercise of virtue / Then fools' approval stings, and honour stains" sorts of situations. But in that case, if the average amount of that feeling of rugpulled betrayedness is that predictable in advance, why not instead just internalize that feeling of rugpulled betrayedness in advance, and stop having such confidences? Surely a lesser degree of confident normativity would have almost all the same positive motivational and hortatory-to-others effects if you're right, without nearly as much of the downsides if you're wrong?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
> Your entire argument ignores

No.

Parapsychologists and the surrounding statisticians who criticize them, over the last half-dozen decades, have put a great deal of work in methodology, as well as meta-work involving trying to take into account exactly the kinds of philosophical arguments about methodology you refer to, into coming up with consensus-agreeable rules for when to declare an effect present or absent. It is precisely according to those agreed-upon rules that the effects taper off and return to equivocality. It is, in fact, your argument that ignores those facts, in the sense that it is unaware of them.

> the insights of philosophers of science who long ago showed it would always be possible to create an infinite number of interpretations of any given dataset

That's part of why I said "I mean, once one has barred fallacies of post-hoc analysis, e.g. using Bonferroni corrections or Bayesian hyperprior regularization". (And here I was worried I was being excessively verbose, and that the social outcome I actually needed to be protecting myself against with my choices of words was the social outcome of someone getting mad at me for taking up too much space, rather than the social outcome of me not having a defense ready to try to slow down someone confabulating grounds for an accusation that I was ignorant.) That is exactly the kind of problem that those statistical techniques for barring fallacies of post hoc analysis are supposed to be about, and the datasets in parapsychology are specifically gathered and organized according to rules as to make techniques like that both applicable and tractable.

I don't know what you must be imagining as "It takes a few decades for the dogmatic materialists to take notice" and "come up with their counterargument", in such a way as to make parapsychology experiments themselves stop replicating. What I see is numbers that start out positive and then go to zero over that timespan. Nobody needs to re-analyze anything or come up with alternative hypotheses to observe that the same experimental paradigm, same competing families of hypotheses, and same form of analysis on the results, that used to produce significant results, upon later repetition stops producing significant results, often in such a way as to dilute even the exciting-seeming initial results into ambiguity. What would be the point of causal vulnerability in that process, whereby anyone's act of coming up with any counterarguments somehow becomes causally relevant to any of the steps of that process, as your competing position would seem to require (other than via causal mechanisms that are themselves supernatural)?

The cases where "coming up with a counterargument" is a thing are cases where everything is free-form, there's lots of interacting layers of selection effect and interpretation, and it's not clear what the relative strengths are of all the intersecting grounds to have expected or not expected anything. For example, skeptics analyzing the Miracle of the Sun at Fatima and seeing if they can get similar subjective phenomena from groups of people staring at the sun or other bright discs -- see e.g. the relatively even-handed https://www.astralcodexten.com/p/the-fatima-sun-miracle-much-more and https://www.astralcodexten.com/p/highlights-from-the-comments-on-fatima , which discuss a lot of confounding points of interpretation like that on both sides.

Also, as a personal point, I think you don't understand how painful it is for me to be in a position where one relatively face-value-plausible body of evidence with its partisans is screaming at me that this situation has huge stakes and, given those stakes, I'm a morally worthless person unless I anticipate consequences as though the supernatural is real even where it would be not-interpret-awayable by materialists, and a different quite face-value-plausible opposite body of evidence with its different group of partisans is screaming at me that this situation has huge stakes and, given those stakes, I'm a morally worthless person unless I anticipate consequences effectively as though the supernatural is not real at all. When I say "exciting-seeming initial results", it is in the same sense as saying that Charlie Brown thinks Lucy might actually not yank the football away from him this time. I'm kind of dissociated and burnt out on it all.

comments.title: Let’s suppose
comments.content:
Let’s suppose for a moment that man’s greatest fear is that the world, in and of itself, is actually meaningless. Suppose all of the things that we fill out lives with are actually just distractions from the truth that we live in a meaningless empty void. The question then becomes, how do we find a purpose and direction for our consciousness? What do we do with our lives?

From the time we are conceived we form attachments to people, places, things, and ideas. Do these things have a reality apart from our own interpretation or is it our attachment that gives them reality in our own minds? One can certainly say that we project value onto them by our own actions. Others can observe our actions and imply that the object of our actions must have a real value or else why would we bother. But what are they really seeing? Is it the real object as I see it, or is their interpretation of the real object colored by the actions of myself and others?

How does one determine where to focus one’s attention when “reality” is so subjective?

I will end this thought here since this is my first post on this site and I am not sure if this is the type of thing which is normally discussed here. I look forward to reading the responses of those who are interested enough to comment.

replies.title: Re: Let’s suppose
replies.content:
No, this isn't really the point of this open post, unless you can find some way to relate it to occultism.

Your supposition is an odd one, and I find it highly unlikely myself -- I don't believe the cosmos has or can have any objective meaning, because "meaning" is not an objective quality. It's a relationship between an experience and a conscious individual experiencer, and the same experience can be meaningful or meaningless to different individuals. Is "Hwæt. We Gardena in geardagum, þeodcyninga, þrym gefrunon, hu ða æþelingas ellen fremedon" meaningful or meaningless? Depends on what you bring to the relationship.

Thus meaning is always something that emerges from a relationship, and it is always subjective. (So is everything else. The claim of "objective reality" is always and only an attempt to privilege one person's subjective experience over others'.) If you have a shortage of meaning in your life, examine your relationships to the various experiences that constitute your world and change them.

replies.title: Re: Let’s suppose
replies.content:
Assuming that we're allowed to respond to off-topic questions, I also disagree with that supposition. At least I personally am not much concerned with the world being meaningful or -less. The closest fear would be what I do being meaningless but that's disconnected from whether the world as a whole has meaning. In a meaningful world, I can choose to live a meaningless life and in a world that isn't meaningful as a whole, human existence can be a meaningful part of it.

I don't really understand the point of the second and third paragraph. Let's say that I want to find a wife and start a family—that feels meaningful to me. As "want" is the operative word in that sentence, this is just an attachment to an idea for now. That attachment is coloured by my environment and my biology: The procreative part is a simple consequence of evolution, the concept of "wife" is a cultural construct, and what I consider to be attractive is partly determined by my genetics, partly by societal beauty standards, and partly by formative experiences in early adolescence. Maybe society has talked me into wanting a regular, monogamous marriage and I'd be happier in a polyamorous relationship. Maybe there's some deeper underlying desire that I really want and this is just how it manifests.

And yet, none of that changes the reality of the attachment. I know it's real because I experience it. Unlike material "reality", which I can only observe by its distorted echo picked up by my senses, it's happening directly in my mind.

This doesn't answer the question of where I should focus my attention on, of course, but that's because the problem was never the subjectivity. I have several such attachments, meaningful things I could imagine myself doing with my life. Some of them are mutually incompatible. But that's a simple (not necessarily easy!) matter of prioritisation.

—David P.

replies.title: Re: An affirmation review request
replies.content: "I am shedding my old self" is a well-formed affirmation. It's in the present tense ("am shedding" rather than "will shed" or "have shed") and it's positive in that it contains no explicit negations ("no, not," etc.). That's all I was saying. If your goal focuses on freeing yourself of something linked to your past, then yes, you do need to acknowledge that in the affirmation!

Of course! I love listening to music. What I don't experience is the crowd effect, which is what makes rock concerts, sports games, and religious revivals so intoxicating to their fans. At a rock concert, the music and especially the rhythm are picked up by the nervous systems of all the fans, bring them into synch, and create a temporary group mind in which the ordinary personality dissolves temporarily. replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
3) Some of the trouble here is terminological: historical Heathens referred to all sorts of apparently non-human beings, some of whom sound an awful lot like faeries, but it's hard to say. With that amount of salt added, though, a few thoughts:
- Claude Lecouteaux has done a lot of good work on Germanic and French folklore and its possible ties to pre-Christian beliefs, much of it at least "next door" to faerie lore, and some more spot on. The most obvious title would be Witches, Werewolves, and Faeries, and the most "Heathen" one might be The Hidden History of Elves and Dwarves. His Encyclopedia of Norse and Germanic Folklore, Mythology, and Magic might also have some good pointers, and Demons and Spirits of the Land might be tangentially relevant.
- Stephen Pollington's The Elder Gods is an excellent and thorough overview of every which kind of spiritual being we have hints of in Old English. Being grounded in England, it might allow for useful comparison with the Celtic and later British sources more commonly associated with Faeries.
- Elves, Witches, and Gods is less scholarly and more focused on current practice, and so might lean more on personal gnosis (I've only skimmed it), but to the degree stuff about Germanic Elves is relevant, I thought I'd mention it
- Not sure if this is as directly relevant, but Imperium Press put out Folktales in the Indo-European Tradition, which gathers traditional folktales by ancestral group (Proto-Celtic, Proto-Germanic, etc) and indicates the ATU index for each story, which might help you to make connections, but sadly, lots of "fairy tales" aren't really about fairies.

That's all that a quick scan of my shelves brings up, but I look forward to seeing what others say.

Cheers,
Jeff

comments.title: On the Significance of Place in the Severing of a Bond
comments.content:
Dear JMG,

Ripley, my dog, is approaching the end of her life, and I’m trying to understand whether the locus of her passing carries any real consequence within the subtle architecture of things. She’s not afraid of the vet; this isn’t a question about comfort. My concern is with the nature of our bond and what unfolds, energetically and psychically, when that bond is severed, depending on where it occurs.

For years she has functioned as an external stabilizer for me, an anchor of instinct, joy, and coherence during a long period of psychic strain. As she declines, my psyche has already begun the work of withdrawal and internalization: dismantling projections (dreams), reclaiming regulation, and reshaping identity as the roles she carried return to me. It feels deliberate, but initiated from deeper layers rather than consciously chosen.

From your understanding, does the location of a deeply bonded companion’s death matter? Is a passing within the home distinct from a passing in a neutral space like the vet? And beyond my own psyche: does the locus of death influence the trajectory or orientation of Ripley’s spirit once it’s unbound from her body?

I’m not looking for symbolic comfort. I’m asking about the mechanics: whether the site of transition has genuine implications for both the human psyche and the departing spirit.

Also, I vaguely recall old MM queries about the duration between death and the departure of the spirit from the body. Can you remind me about this, and is it the same for non-human animals?

Finally, just thank you. You've made a significant and definitely positive impact on my life, and likely many others.

replies.title: Re: On the Significance of Place in the Severing of a Bond
replies.content:
The place is irrelevant. It would be a very good thing if you are close to Ripley when she passes, as it'll make the transition much more comfortable for her to know that you're with her, but the other details don't matter in the least.

The process is the same for any mammal as it is for a human being. As the material body shuts down, the soul and the other bodies drift loose from it; clairvoyants can see what looks like a silver cord connecting the soul in its other bodies from the material body. Once the material body actually dies, the cord dissolves and the soul moves out of our ordinary three-dimensional space. For a nonhuman animal, this is easier than it is for a human being (or the handful of other creatures that also have mental sheaths), because they still have collective oversouls on the species level -- that's the source of what scientists call "instinct." The oversoul will gather up Ripley and help her through the process of shedding her etheric body, processing her life experiences, and preparing for her next incarnation.

Finally, please accept my condolences. That's a difficult thing to go through.

replies.title: Re: On the Significance of Place in the Severing of a Bond
replies.content:
My condolences as well. I have a dog in his late middle age, and if he sticks around another three years I'll be lucky. He's been a steadfast companion, and I expect his passing will be difficult for me. I have many of the same concerns as you, about the ramifications of his passing.

-Cliff

comments.title: Natural magic and queries about occultism
comments.content:
Hi John, I wanted to tell you and the other people who might read this about my experience with natural magic and ask you some questions.

My dad lent me your book on natural magic and I found it extremely helpful! The first amulet I made was the protective amulet made of kosher salt, a shiny nail surrounded by red cloth.

That amulet seems to have given me perfect sleep. Normally, I have a lot of trouble getting asleep and waking up. With the amulet, I fall asleep fast and wake up rested. I know that it’s the amulets doing because that has not happened in years. I had perfect sleep for the two weeks I wore it.

The amulet also seems to be helping with the SoP. My visualizations during the closing were stronger.
Naturally, when I noticed the effects of this simple amulet, I made another one. This time I used Jupiter in Leo as my correspondence (I wanted some help with my job-hunting). I combined crushed almonds, saffron, and frankincense in a purple bag during the day and hour of Jupiter, said a prayer where asked him to bless my amulet and offered him some traditional Swedish glögg. It’s wine infused with spices, usually enjoyed in the winter, served hot with almonds in it.

That amulet seems to be working well too. The day after I made it, I got a strong hunch to apply for a job I normally wouldn’t feel confident going for. An hour later I got an email inviting me to an interview the following Monday. That interview went well, and now I’ve been called to a second one. That interview was today and I got the job!

I don’t know much of this is me using the amulets as affirmations and the amulets affecting consciousness in accordance with their will, but either way I like the results!

Here are my questions:
1) When I made the second amulet, I discarded the protective one because I wasn’t sure how they might interact. Are the amulets alright to mix? I want my perfect sleep back (haha).

2) Is it appropriate to bless your amulet multiple times to make it even stronger?

3) What’s your opinion on meditating more than once a day? There is so much to meditate on, the thought of dedicating an entire day to meditation and occult study seems really cool to me.

4) How do you go about taking on the work of multiple orders? Do you do them in sequence or do you do them in parallel? I’m specifically thinking of doing the work of the Golden section and FHR.

replies.title: Re: Natural magic and queries about occultism
replies.content:
1) Of course you can have more than one amulet, as long as they don't have conflicting purposes -- for example, don't make one amulet to help you be more kind and sympathetic and another to make you more fierce and competitive! The protective amulet will go with just about anything.

2) By all means experiment with this and see how it works for you.

3) Beginners often want to do this. It's not a good idea, because it can make you spacy and unable to function well in the rest of your life. For beginners, 15 minutes once a day is a good maximum.

4) One at a time, always, so you can focus on each order's lessons!

replies.title: Re: Natural magic and queries about occultism
replies.content:
Thank you! Jupiter will definitely receive more Glögg in the future.

Also, this might be a very hard question to answer, but how do you measure the success of an amulet that you've made?

It would be cool to develop the understanding of natural magic so you could go "this needs more saffron" but I have a hard time seeing how you get there.

replies.title: Re: Natural magic and queries about occultism
replies.content: You measure the success of an amulet by observing the results. If you make an amulet to give yourself spiritual protection, and sleep well for the first time in months, I'd say that was a success!

replies.title: Re: Natural magic and queries about occultism
replies.content: Thank you for sharing your amulet experience. I just made and blessed my first one-- I hope it will be as beneficent as yours!

comments.content:
I have a question about the Psalms.

I’m an Orthodox Christian, and for centuries it has been standard practice for monastics in our church to chant and pray the entire Psalter regularly, in some cases daily.

In recent years a version of this has trickled down to the laity. It has become popular for Orthodox churches to form lay Psalter reading groups in which individuals commit to praying sections of the Psalter nightly during the church fasting periods, mainly Advent and Lent, usually cycling through the whole Psalter twice in a 40 day fasting period.

I’ve now participated 3 times in my church’s fasting period Psalter groups, and the uneasiness I’ve felt about the practice since the beginning has never gone away. There are individual Psalms that I love beyond words and that are precious and powerful to me, and are worth meditating on, and yes, praying.

But I have been unable to get over my alarm and wariness around praying the imprecatory Psalms that are, quite frankly, curses. It feels all wrong. My sense of wrongness and danger increases with each round.

This opinion of mine is heavily taboo within the church, and the standard response to qualms like mine is to say it isn’t cursing at all, but rather all metaphorical, and the “enemies” that the imprecatory Psalms are calling down destruction upon are not people but abstract sins.

I don’t buy it.

My sense right now is that this Psalter prayer practice might possibly have merit within the context of monasticism and dedicated ascetic life, but as lay people it’s playing with fire. I committed to joining the current Advent group, and am in the prayer list and supposed to be praying my assigned psalms nightly, but I’ve not been fully participating.

My questions:

1.) Do you have any insight into or opinions regarding the dangers and risks (or benefits) of Psalter prayer practices that include the imprecatory Psalms? Do you think my intuitive revulsion towards the cursing Psalms, and my sense they are real curses (not abstract “struggles against sin”) is correct?

2.) I agreed to participate in this Advent group, and I’m not. Any thoughts on blowback from that or ways of mitigating it? I’ve considered praying nightly for everyone on the list, but not praying the full Psalter.

Thank you!

1) Oh, they're curses, all right. Two of the magical traditions in which I'm initiated include the Psalms as magical resources, and there are quite a few of them that I won't use under any circumstances.

2) I think praying nightly for everyone on the list, and choosing one Psalm with which you're entirely comfortable and praying it nightly as well, would be a good choice. Next year, though, you'll want to beg off.

This is partly because this is the only version I can sing, and I do prefer to chant the Psalms rather than recite them.  I spent a few years in the choir of in an Anglo-Catholic parish which emphasized plainchant settings along with the standard hymns, so this is what comes naturally to me.  I've been curious about using the Masoretic or Septuagint versions, but it's really not something I could see myself doing as a practical everyday matter.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Does anything go wrong if you just interpolate a bunch of phrases like "but only insofar as this can be beneficially understood as applying metaphorically to sins or something, rather than to the people that other people might have used this psalm to curse" everywhere? You know, to hold your church's feet to the fire about whether or not they really stand behind the whole implied moral-justification structure behind their claims. (Sort of a "It is you who have said it" thing. If you wanted to be especially passive-aggressive, you could also interpolate ", you know, the way thus-and-such community would get mad at me if I insisted wasn't necessarily going to happen by default", except I guess "while reading an imprecatory psalm" might not be so much of a safe time and place to be passive-aggressive and talk about themes of anger.)

I mean, assuming that they don't just turn around and instead get mad about the tabooness of "faithlessly"/"heterodoxically" even supposing that there even *was* any risk of those psalms having effects like that, enough for the hedging to be justified.

(I'm not sure the thing about cursing sins even works; sometimes the correct metaphorical frame is that you can't destroy a wound by stabbing it a lot. One alternative spin would be for the psalms to be for contemplating the incomprehensible greatness of the god who could *in principle* inflict such punishments, or something. I guess the interpolations you'd need in that case would be a little more awkward linguistically.)

I don't know much about Greek Orthodox stories about saints, so I don't know if there's useful stories there to argue from, like the one in the Talmud about Rabbi Eliezer's wife distracting him during prayer so that his anger at her brother Rabbi Gamaliel wouldn't get in contact with divine force and cause Gamaliel to be struck dead, his crops scourged, and/or his eyes burned, etc. I guess the Bible has the story about Elisha off-handedly cursing some mocking children and then they get mauled by bears, and the part in 2 Maccabees where the prayers of the people to stop Heliodorus from profaning the temple treasury lead to Heliodorus being struck unconscious by a vision for so long that the high priest feels that he has to makes a sacrifice for his recovery to forestall imperial suspicion of foul play. The implied principle being, I don't know, something about how there are rules governing how curse-impulse propagates and what levers it flips when it reaches them, and it would be easy to miscalculate the consequences there if you weren't expecting things like what happened in those two stories.

comments.title: Petrus Apianus Diagram
comments.content:
Hello, this may be a dumb question, but I'm looking at this diagram of the Ptolomaic cosmology by Petrus Apianus, and I'm looking at the 3 zodiacs in the outside three circles - the 8th, 9th, and 10th spheres. Am I off base by interpreting the 8th sphere zodiac as the sidereal zodiac, based on the fixed stars, the 9th sphere zodiac as the tropical zodiac, and the 10th sphere zodiac as some Ideal zodiac outside of space and time?

Here's the picture: https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Ptolemaicsystem-small.png

replies.title: Re: Petrus Apianus Diagram
replies.content: It's a little more technical than that. In Ptolemaic astronomy, any movement of the heavens has to be assigned to its own sphere. The 8th sphere is the sphere of the not-quite-fixed stars -- they knew by the Middle Ages that the stars actually shift a little, so it has to be a sphere. The zodiacal figures there represent the constellations. The ninth sphere is the sphere responsible for the precession of the equinoxes -- you can see the equinoctial points marked by circles crosses in Pisces and Virgo. The tenth sphere is the one responsible for the overall movement of the heavens, spinning around the earth in 24 hours. It's outside the tenth that you get the timeless COELUM EMPYREUM HABITACULUM DEI ET OMNIUN ELECTORUM, the empyrean heaven, the habitation of God and all the elect/saved.

comments.title: (no subject)
comments.content:
John--

I find myself wrestling periodically with a profound sense of futility which I suspect is being driven in some part by a diffusion of energy (in the sense of lacking focus/direction, dissipating into the void). Periods of stress, usually unplanned/difficult circumstances, provoke a sense of powerlessness and futility, which in turn provoke a sense of insignificance and futility. Put in Christian terms: in the body of Christ there are differing parts with differing functions and designs--eyes and feet, for example--and I often feel that I have been relegated to the role of left pinky toe...in which case, why even bother? I realize much of this is mood--and I hate being subject to moods, another issue of control. How can I stop these thoughts and prevent the spiral from occurring? Are there any tools or practices that you would recommend? Prayer has had limited success thus far and I am frustrated with still having to deal with this nonsense.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
You need to start by finding out why they're there. You can't get rid of obsessive thoughts by denial or white-knuckling it -- you have to find the root.

Feelings of futility, like those of depression, are especially complex because they're tertiary emotions. Basically, you have a primary emotion -- usually shame, fear, or grief -- from childhood experiences you weren't able to deal with at the time. Since those are very hard for a child to face, they usually get suppressed and hidden beneath a secondary emotion of anger, like an unhealed wound beneath a hard protective scab. If it's impossible for the child to express anger openly, the anger is then suppressed in its turn -- and so much life energy is locked up that way that feelings of depression, futility, emptiness, and the like then become the default setting of the psyche.

So you've got a challenging task ahead of you. First you have to let yourself experience your unresolved anger, which can be a rough road all by itself. Then you have to get beneath the anger to the primary emotion or emotions underneath, relive those experiences, and deal with the emotions. It's a lot of work! One set of tools I've found useful in dealing with such things are the exercises of the Order of Spiritual Alchemy, which can be downloaded free of charge here:

https://octagonsociety.org/preliminary-lessons/

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
In addition to the recommendations given by JMG you might want to try out two other methods if you're interested:

Since you wrote that you experience spiraling thoughts you could experiment with the following for such times in which you need to function and don't have time to either fall into ruminative thought or deal with it in a reflective way:

1) Give thanks to the source of the thought

When you get an anxious or obsessive thought, acknowledge it and say internally something like: "Thanks mind, I am taking care of this. You don't need to worry." (You can name the instance "mind" anything else that's appropriate and not derogatory.)

2) Let thoughts drift away

Similarly, when you get such a thought you can imagine placing it on a moving object (something like a leave on a river that is flowing in front of you) and watch it drift out of your area of awareness.

These two techniques come out of the toolbox of ACT (Acceptance and Commitment Therapy). Their aim is what they call defusion: Breaking the identification with the thought. I have used them with great success over the years and found them very helpful. These will best work in addition to addressing the root as JMG already mentioned.

comments.title: Eugene Fersen's Science of Being
comments.content:
Hello, JMG,

Science of Being – anon reporting. This week I’m finishing working through the second part of the Science of Being correspondence course, concerned with mental well-being and development. A few notes on the exercises covered:

Attention + Concentration: rather self-explanatory set of exercises, with a few idiosyncrasies. Instructions for attention exercise suggest treating everything one encounters as a message, and trying to figure out its meaning, which is fun, when treated as a game, but can take an unhealthy turn if overdone, I imagine. For concentration exercise, Fersen suggests picking a concept of an object, which is an odd middle ground between concentration on a symbol and concentration on a concrete objects.

Fertilization: for this exercise Fersen suggests concentrating on the solar plexus after making contact with the Universal Life Energy. This is supposed to purify and fix one’s subconsciousness, of which solar plexus is treated a seat. In the accompanying lesson Fersen describes the subconscious as a hellish realm, reminiscent of the lower astral as described by some occultists, and this exercise, combined with positive thinking, is supposed to help filtering out negative thoughts trying to rise up into the conscious part of the mind.

Thought Projection: a variation of the Concentration exercise, followed up by a deep in-breath and letting the concentrated thought go with the out-breath. This technique is used in later exercises, but on its own, Fersen claims, it can be used to deliver telepathic messages. It can also be used for memorization, by sending the concentrated thought on the information to be memorized to one’s subconscious through the solar plexus.

Observation: an odd exercise that borders discursive meditation and psychometry. It consists of concentration on a physical object and following the train of thoughts concerning object’s history. The accompanying lesson contains instructions for character reading, which consist of making contact with the ULE and listening to one’s intuition regarding the person being read.

Realization: like Observation exercise, it consists of following a train of thoughts, but this time a practical problem serves as an object of concentration.

Visualization: a technique Fersen suggests using instead of working with affirmations, it consists of visualizing the desired result and then sending the thought to one’s subconscious through the solar plexus.

Materialization: somewhat weirdly named, this exercise is a variation of the previous one, serving as a substitute for affirmations along the lines of “I find and make use of the opportunities to make money.”

Invigoration: a follow up to Fertilization exercise, this one consists of making contact with the ULE and directing it toward the pituitary gland, which is treated as a seat of consciousness, with intention of purifying and fixing one’s conscious mind. The accompanying lesson contains the instruction for using ULE for healing. Magnetic healing consists of making contact with the ULE and directing it through the fingers toward the patient, or an object serving as a poppet, in case of absent treatment. Mental healing is similar, but using the character reading technique to diagnose an underlying psychological problem, which, as is usual with the New Thought teachings, is a real cause of the disease, and directing ULE toward fixing it.

Polarization: again, somewhat oddly named, this exercise consists of concentrating on a thought of oneness with the Universal Mind and directing ULE towards the pineal gland, which is treated as a point of contact with the superconscious. This exercise is supposed to facilitate development of intuition.

Overall, Science of Being is a mixed bag so far. On one hand, some of Fersen’s advice is sound and mirrors yours, like pointing out the necessity of grounding one’s working (or use of creative visualization, in Fersen’s case) by taking immediate actions towards the goal on the physical plane, or distinguishing real intuition from a product of the conscious mind by its emotional effect. And some of his techniques worked fairly well for me, for example, Realization exercise helped me a lot to get through a difficult journaling topic. But on the other hand, Fersen himself comes off as a rather untrustworthy character. Obviously, most of his teachings didn’t originate with him, a lot of it is fairly standard New Thought, likely compiled from his contemporaries’ material, there are also some hints of him being familiar with Cabala in 1923 book version, and then there are some idiosyncrasies witch are probably his own, but tracing what came from where is beyond my present knowledge of occultism.

Next week I’m starting the third and last part of the correspondence course, concerned with the spiritual training. Last time, when I took a peek at the exercises given there, they read like a word salad, so soon I will see whether they would make sense in context.

Thank you for hosting Magic Mondays, and best wishes to you and fellow posters!

comments.title: Ponerology for The Archdruid!!!
comments.content:
(Sven E.)

Was me doing the Stephen Hayes-posting, Crowley-posting, and the Lobaczewski-posting last week. Sometimes my clandestine nature gets the better of me, and I forget to sign my poasts ;-) Anyway, you asked for an accessible English source on the latter. Here you go:

https://www.survivorshandbook.com/wp-content/articles/political-ponerology.pdf

I know you don't like pirated books, but the powers that be have put quite some effort in getting this baby out of circulation. Also, the author is long since departed, and I suspect he would gleefully rub his hands if someone were to put his work into the hands of a based Archdruid. As a longtime Toynbee-reader, I think you will find much to chew on in the latter part of the book. He goes into great detail with regards to the dominant minority ("pathocracy" in his terms) and how its road to ruin plays out as a process. One very astute observation that comes at the beginning of part 2, is the severe cognitive dissonance that results from the PMC's incessant efforts to convince themselves that they are somehow morally superior to those they are parasitizing, and since this effort (cuz futile) has to be all-encompassing, eventually leads to a debasement of consciousness so thorough that in the end nothing can be allowed to follow naturally or logically.

The weakest part of the book is part 1. Here he tries to come up with a metaphysics to account for the arising of sickly consciousness, and just ends up in handwaving about it having to have been caused by its physical manifestations ("Stalin was psycho, must have been caused by brain damage, which means he must have been dropped on the head, which means he was cuz he was psycho, so his momma therefore dropped him on the head, causing him to be psycho"), it's the standard 80s materialist cum-hoc-ergo-propter-hoc stick. But I think he felt he needed to come up with a way to talk about matters that transcended the emotional-moralistic (the "tyranny of the natural worldview" in his terms), and that was the best he could do. He really tries to find a way to grapple with evil (cognitive collapse) from a vantage point on the mental plane, and once past his initial fumblings, he really starts batting them out of the ballpark...

Hope this helps with regards to your current project. You might not be aware of it, but there is a whole internet subculture dedicated to the same topic, and there are some serious dudes breaking ground there. For starters, I think many would love to hear you on Harrison Koehli's podcast. That guy has been confronting the void with pickaxe and shovel for years. He hosted your pal Arthur Versluis a while back.

I'll give it a thorough read once it arrives, and see what I think. The discussion of cognitive dissonance promises to be well worth close study!

comments.title: Collective Karma and Migration
comments.content:
How do you feel about collective karma, JMG? Personally, I hate the idea; I recoil from it in horror. I'm programmed to do so as an individualist, Faustian soul. Which doesn't mean it doesn't happen.

I'm looking at what is currently happening in Canada, my home. If there's collective karma associated with a nation-state, our karma would be related to the peaceful demographic replacement of the native tribes who were foolish enough to let Europeans onto their land. Which we are absolutely seeing-- a peaceful demographic replacement by people our leaders are inviting in. An overwhelming percentage of them happen to be from India, which makes me wonder if Canada's connection to the British Empire gives us some of their collective global karma. It seems a bit too pat, though.

Sure, the model fits Canada, and to some extent the UK-- but America? Brazil? I'm not sure see it elsewhere. Maybe the chickens haven't come home to roost uniformly. Is there a timescale tradition suggests one can expect that sort of thing, or is it one of those "know neither the day or the hour" sort of things?

At what scale does collective karma operate anyway, do you think? I've heard it talked about in terms of families. Does it accumulate to neighborhoods, cities, states and countries? I figure it's got to be fractal in some way. The Faustian Man in me wants to rail against the unfairness of it, but then I suppose our individual souls must be born into societies whose collective karma aligns with our personal karma.

I'm also wondering what I can do to work through said karma, be it collective or individual. I'm going to be honest: I already find people stiflingly difficult to deal with when I share a background with them. When I don't, there's just all sorts of extra points for friction and confusion that it seems not even worth trying. Perhaps I need to learn the lessons of multiculturalism, because I'd be much more comfortable in a monocultural ethnic enclave.

I'm not sure if its a lesson I desperately need to learn this incarnation or if I'm just not meant to be a "people person" this time around and should focus on solitary pursuits. Would that show up in my chart if I could find a good natal astrologer, I wonder?

replies.title: Re: Collective Karma and Migration
replies.content:
Collective karma certainly exists, but it doesn't affect everybody equally. The more mass-minded you are, the more of your thoughts come from your community (and these days, the media) rather than from independent reflection, the more likely you are to be swept up in collective karma. There's a lot of it to go around these days, of course.

As for you, the fact that you're not a "people person" may shield you from the worst of the collective karma of your nation. Yes, that'll be all over your natal chart.

replies.title: Re: A query that is at a tangent to the plane curves 1.) "gifted" children, and 2.) affirmations
replies.content:
1) I don't know. I was supposed to go into a gifted program in one of my elementary schools, but my father insisted otherwise, so I got bullied and beaten in the usual way by average kids. If it did me any good I have to say I'm still not aware of it.

comments.title: Paganism getting started.

Hello Mr Greer

After a long attempt at Christianity I'm coming to realize that me and the Christian God do not get along (To all the Christians this is absolutely not meant as an insult and I hope your eternity in the new heavens and earth is everlasting and joyful.) I was always a weird Christian and set off a couple of pastors with my habit of making sacrifices before hunting or fishing. Now for actually practicing I've only found reconstructionists who are very obsessed with figuring out exactly how the Romans sacrificed to Jupiter. And while reconstruction is all well and good I am not going to be buying a toga. From my own reading of the anthropology of paganism the normal way of doing thing is a combination of received wisdom for what to do and making everything else up as you go along. Which now that I think about it is remarkably similar to practicing magic. So is getting started as simple as pick a god make a prayer in good faith and either try and find a ritual or cook on up and see if it works? Or is there a better way of doing things.

Have a blessed day.

replies.title: Re: Paganism getting started.
replies.content: Yes, that's basically how it works. The gods have their preferences but I can't see any reason to think that they're even half as obsessive about the fine details of worship as, say, your average Pagan Reconstructionist. You talk to the gods and then listen to them, you make offerings that seem appropriate, and you build a relationship with them the way you'd build a relationship with any older, wiser, and mightier friend. Right now I'm sketching out a book on the subject -- I think there's ample interest -- but no need to wait for that; reach out to the gods and see what happens.
